query: efficacy of benralizumab in chronic spontaneous urticaria

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Indications -- Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA has approved omalizumab for treating chronic spontaneous urticaria in adolescents 12 or older, as well as adults, who still experience symptoms even after receiving the H1 antihistamine treatment. However, this drug is not approved for other types of urticaria.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Urticaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Omalizumab is a monoclonal antibody with a high affinity for free IgE. Given as a subcutaneous injection, the standard dosing regimen is 300 mg every 4 weeks. This has been shown to be efficacious as a second-line adjunct therapy for CSU that is unresponsive to H1-antihistamines. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Urticaria -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients in whom symptoms remain refractory to H1-antihistamines should be referred to a dermatologist and/or clinical immunologist for review and initiation of second and third-line therapeutic agents. CSU follows a fluctuant disease course and may resolve spontaneously. Accordingly, efficacy and need for continuation of treatment should be evaluated periodically at 3- to- 6-month intervals. [1] The psychological burden of CSU should not be underestimated, and where appropriate, counseling and psychotherapy may enhance overall health outcomes. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Omalizumab received initial approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 2003 for the treatment of moderate-to-severe asthma. Subsequently, in 2014, the drug was also approved for use in patients with chronic idiopathic or spontaneous urticaria in the United States and Europe. Omalizumab is a recombinant humanized immunoglobulin G1 (IgG1) monoclonal anti-IgE antibody utilized to treat allergic asthma and chronic urticaria. This medication works by interacting with the high-affinity receptor Fc-epsilon-RI, typically found on eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils, therefore playing a critical role in preventing the allergic cascade. As a result, omalizumab plays a vital role in managing moderate-to-severe IgE-mediated asthma and, more recently, in treating chronic urticaria. This activity will comprehensively cover the indications, contraindications, mechanisms of action, pharmacokinetics, adverse effects, warnings, precautions, and monitoring requirements of omalizumab. In addition, this activity will also provide updates on some of the critical studies implemented primarily for asthma and include information on urticarial reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Urticaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Ciclosporin is recommended as a third-line agent for CSU that remains refractory to the combination of H1-antihistamine and omalizumab. CSU is an off-label indication, and the dose and duration of ciclosporin should be minimized to avoid adverse events, including nephrotoxicity and hypertension. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Omalizumab received initial approval from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 2003 for treating moderate-to-severe asthma. Subsequently, in 2014, the drug was also approved for use in patients with chronic idiopathic or spontaneous urticaria in the United States and Europe. Omalizumab is the first drug licensed to treat chronic urticaria in patients who still experience symptoms despite receiving H1-antihistamine treatment. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate patients for omalizumab therapy based on indications, such as moderate-to-severe asthma or chronic urticaria. Screen patients thoroughly for eligibility, including total serum IgE levels, potential contraindications, and medical history, before initiating omalizumab treatment. Assess patients' responses to omalizumab treatment by monitoring their symptom improvement, exacerbation frequency, and quality of life measures and adjusting the therapy as needed. Communicate with patients effectively about omalizumab therapy, explaining its benefits, potential risks, and expected outcomes and addressing their concerns or questions. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action of omalizumab differs for allergic asthma and chronic urticaria.

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: There is evidence supporting the notion that patients with chronic urticaria have an abnormal basophil IgE high-affinity receptor, and it is also observed that basophils are often found in chronic urticarial lesion sites. Another evidence suggests that omalizumab treatment can reverse basopenia and increase IgE receptor expression, typically observed in patients with chronic urticaria. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dupilumab -- Indications -- Non-FDA Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non-FDA-approved indications include allergic contact dermatitis, hand dermatitis, chronic spontaneous urticaria, and alopecia areata. [1] [14] Extensive literature suggests the efficacy of dupilumab in treating conditions characterized by Th2-mediated inflammation. [15] Clinical investigations are currently being conducted to assess the effectiveness of dupilumab in treating autoimmune blistering diseases, including bullous pemphigoid and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Urticaria -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: For acute flares of CSU, short courses only of systemic corticosteroids may help alleviate symptom severity and reduce the duration of the flare. [1] Topical corticosteroids do not have a role in CSU. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Solar Urticaria -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Those who fail conservative treatment may be managed with phototherapy and biological agents. Overall, the prognosis for patients with severe urticaria is poor. Many patients are restricted indoors and lead a poor quality of life. [15] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Urticaria -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the proposed etiology of chronic spontaneous urticaria. Summarize the pertinent points to explore in history in the evaluation of patients with chronic spontaneous urticaria. Outline the pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic treatment options available for chronic spontaneous urticaria. Discuss strategies for improving care coordination and communication among the interprofessional team to improve outcomes in the treatment of chronic spontaneous urticaria. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Administration -- Chronic Urticaria
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended dosage for omalizumab for treating chronic urticaria is either 150 or 300 mg  every 4 weeks, depending on the patient's initial serum total IgE level or weight. A retrospective chart review of 43 chronic urticaria patients who received omalizumab between 2006 and 2015 revealed that 37 out of 41 patients (90.2%) responded positively within 3 months duration. [14] Joint guidelines by the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology (AAAAI)/American College of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology (ACAAI) indicate that omalizumab can improve the quality of life, reduce the need for oral corticosteroids, and potentially decrease the need for surgery in chronic urticaria patients. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medications -- Clinical Significance -- Monoclonal Antibody Immune-Modulating Drugs
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab 100 mg given subcutaneously every eight weeks is currently in human trials, but data looks promising for treatment in refractory asthma and COPD. Similar to mepolizumab and reslizumab, this medicine binds to the IL-5 receptor, thus inhibiting its signal to proliferate eosinophils in the bone marrow and augmenting eosinophil apoptosis. The extent of reduction in exacerbations, improvement in FEV1, and symptom reduction seem proportional to the baseline blood eosinophil count. Side Effects Possible side effects of benralizumab include headache, pharyngitis, pyrexia, and various hypersensitivity reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: The concomitant use of omalizumab with immunosuppressive agents has not yet been assessed; therefore, it is advised to avoid this combination. The concomitant use of omalizumab and immunotherapy has not been thoroughly evaluated; therefore, the manufacturer's labeling recommends avoiding this combination. However, a recent study indicated that it could be administered safely. [26] The concomitant use of ACE inhibitors in chronic spontaneous urticaria may reduce omalizumab's efficacy. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab and mepolizumab are both classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating that each of these agents is recognized as a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. This classification groups the two drugs together within the same therapeutic class, highlighting their shared role in managing airway inflammation and improving respiratory outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This grouping indicates that each of these agents is a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions, such as severe asthma, and they share a common therapeutic classification within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system.

Overall, the taxonomy illustrates how individual drugs—ranging from antiallergic nasal agents, plain and decongestant sympathomimetics, adrenergic substances, H1‑histamine antagonists, to specialized therapies for hereditary angioedema and rare musculoskeletal disorders—are organized into overlapping classes that guide precise therapeutic selection and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Antipruritika are agents that alleviate itching, and ketotifen is classified as an antipruritic medication. As a member of the antipruritika group, ketotifen is used to reduce pruritus associated with allergic and dermatologic conditions, often acting as a mast‑cell stabilizer and antihistamine to control histamine‑mediated itch.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Tolnaftate is contraindicated in patients who have the hypersensitivity condition فرط تحسس, meaning it should not be used in individuals with this allergic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2484
retrieved_docs.content: Fenoprofen and butenafine are both contraindicated in the presence of hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Use of fenoprofen is not recommended for patients who have a hypersensitivity reaction, and similarly, butenafine should be avoided in individuals with hypersensitivity disease. Recognizing hypersensitivity as a contraindicating factor for these medications is essential for safe prescribing and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases”. These agents are used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, and they represent a distinct therapeutic class within the broader ATC framework. Fenspiride is listed both as a member of this systemic group and as a member of the inhalant group “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC”, indicating that it can be administered by inhalation as well as having systemic activity. Within the inhalant category, the subclass “Antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excl. corticosteroids” is defined as a type of “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC”, highlighting non‑corticosteroid antiallergic inhaled therapies. This classification clarifies the relationships among systemic biologics, inhaled agents, and specific antiallergic inhalants, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate treatments based on route of administration and pharmacologic class.
